title: The Study of Wave Interactions: Where Beautiful Mathematical Ideas Come Together III
speaker: Gigliola Staffilani
abstract: Phenomena involving interactions of waves happen at different scales and in different media: from gravitational waves to the waves on the surface of the ocean, from our milk and coffee in the morning to infinitesimal particles that behave like wave packets in quantum physics. These phenomena are difficult to study in a rigorous mathematical manner, but maybe because of this challenge mathematicians have developed interdisciplinary approaches that are powerful and beautiful.

proof_stages.label: Wave-turbulence kinetic-limit problem
proof_stages.summary: The lecture formulates wave turbulence as a statistical theory of many weakly interacting waves, introduces the large-box and weak-nonlinearity limits at kinetic time, and contrasts this probabilistic energy-spectrum question with the deterministic cascade discussed previously.

proof_stages.label: Formal three-wave kinetic derivation
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains the physicists' formal recipe, instantiates it for the Zakharov-Kuznetsov-type equation, writes the quadratic collision operator, and derives it heuristically by a random-phase/amplitude Duhamel expansion truncated at order epsilon squared with simple Feynman diagrams.

proof_stages.label: Rigorous kinetic-limit precedents
proof_stages.summary: Prior rigorous derivations for random linear Schrödinger equations, equilibrium random NLS, continuum cubic NLS, and dissipative ZK are reviewed to locate the remaining difficulties for a nonlinear, non-equilibrium, lattice ZK theorem.

proof_stages.label: Stochastic lattice ZK theorem
proof_stages.summary: The central model is introduced as a lattice ZK equation with weak nonlinearity and vanishing energy-preserving stochastic forcing, and the theorem states convergence of the rescaled two-point correlation at kinetic time to a three-wave kinetic equation.

proof_stages.label: Singular dispersion and angular smoothing
proof_stages.summary: Because the ZK dispersion relation vanishes on a whole hyperplane, resonant interactions cannot be controlled by ordinary oscillatory estimates, so the noise is designed to act on angular variables and the induced density/Fokker-Planck equation supplies smoothing and a priori bounds.

proof_stages.label: All-order graph expansion and graph separation
proof_stages.summary: A Duhamel/Feynman-graph expansion is carried to a depth growing with epsilon, after which ladder graphs are isolated as the leading contribution producing the collision operator and all nonleading graphs must be estimated away using the noise and crossing-type bounds.

proof_stages.label: Obstructions and resonance broadening
proof_stages.summary: Three obstacles are analyzed: flat-dispersion counterexamples to crossing estimates, the lack of decay caused by the quadratic ZK nonlinearity, and the failure of the naive resonant delta measure, the last being repaired through resonance broadening.

proof_stages.label: Outlook beyond the theorem
proof_stages.summary: The lecture closes with open directions concerning the relation to deterministic energy cascades, times beyond epsilon to the minus two, quantum and homogeneous wave kinetic equations, other dispersive systems, and the effect of noise or lattice choices.

examples.name: Flat-dispersion crossing-estimate counterexample
examples.note: The counterexample for dispersions vanishing on affine hyperplanes shows why standard crossing estimates fail and motivates the noise-assisted, weaker limiting strategy.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker checks how the flat/singular dispersion falls on hyperplanes, explains why crossing estimates fail there, and traces how noise/resonance broadening are used to overcome that obstruction.
